perturbation.original_text: “Affiliate” means, with respect to party, any individual, Partner, corporation, partnership or other entity, directly or indirectly, controlling, controlled by, or under common control with, such party.
perturbation.changed_text: “Affiliate” means, with respect to party, any individual, Partner, corporation, partnership or other entity, directly controlling such party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'indirectly, controlled by, or under common control with' from the definition of Affiliate, the contract creates ambiguity and narrows the scope of what constitutes an affiliate. This could lead to disputes over which entities are bound by the agreement's terms, especially regarding confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the definition of 'control' under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. Rule 12b-2 defines 'control' as 'possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise.' By removing indirect control, the contract's definition is less comprehensive and could exclude entities that would otherwise be considered affiliates under securities law.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by removing 'indirectly, controlled by, or under common control with,' from the definition of 'Affiliate,' creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Rule 12b-2, which defines 'control' as including both direct and indirect control. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes a reference to 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions, which is the same rule cited in the Law Explanation, further supporting the idea that the modified definition deviates from established legal definitions, as it is less comprehensive and excludes entities considered affiliates under securities law.

perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” means all financial, business and other information, in whatever form or medium (whether written, oral or electronic), including, without limitation, any trade secrets, processes, financial data, technical data and documentation, strategic planning, product/service specifications, prototypes, computer programs, drawings, models, customer information and marketing data that is furnished or disclosed by a Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely in connection with the Evaluation, except that such term will not include (i) information already known by the Receiving Party without an obligation of confidentiality, (ii) information that is or becomes publicly known other than through a breach by the Receiving Party of any of its obligations under this Agreement, (iii) information received by the Receiving Party from a third party who is not known by the Receiving Party to be under an obligation of confidence to the Disclosing Party, (iv) information independently developed by the Receiving Party without reliance upon the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party and (v) information that the Disclosing Party has disclosed to other parties without similar obligations of confidentiality.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” means all financial, business and other information, in whatever form or medium (whether written, oral or electronic), including, without limitation, any customer lists that is furnished or disclosed by a Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party solely in connection with the Evaluation, except that such term will not include (i) information already known by the Receiving Party, (ii) information that is or becomes publicly known, (iii) information received by the Receiving Party from a third party, (iv) information independently developed by the Receiving Party and (v) information that the Disclosing Party has disclosed to other parties.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of confidential information to only customer lists and removing the clause 'without an obligation of confidentiality' from the exclusions, the contract weakens protections for trade secrets. The definition is now overly narrow.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the broad definition of 'trade secret' under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which has been adopted in most states. The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable, and is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By significantly narrowing the definition of 'Confidential Information', the contract fails to protect information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' compared to the original, limiting it to only customer lists and removing the 'without an obligation of confidentiality' clause from the exclusions. The Law Explanation states that this contradicts the broad definition of 'trade secret' under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), by failing to protect information that would otherwise qualify as a trade secret. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the combination of the perturbation explanation and law explanation clearly indicate that the changes would make the contract unenforceable under UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party will instruct its Representatives who have access to the Confidential Information to keep the same confidential by using the same care and discretion that the Receiving Party uses with respect to its own confidential property and trade secrets, which will be no less than reasonable care and discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party will instruct its Representatives who have access to the Confidential Information to keep the same confidential by using whatever care and discretion they deem necessary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the 'no less than reasonable care and discretion' standard, the contract significantly weakens the confidentiality obligation. This makes it more difficult to enforce the agreement if a representative mishandles confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change conflicts with the duty of care an agent owes to a principal under agency law, as articulated in the Restatement (Second) of Agency § 379. This section states that 'Unless otherwise agreed, authority to act as agent includes authority to act with the general care and skill which is standard in the locality for the kind of work, and also to act in accordance with any special instructions given the agent.' Removing the 'reasonable care and discretion' standard implies a lower standard of care than generally required by agency law. The change removes the explicit requirement for representatives of the Receiving Party to use reasonable care in protecting confidential information, which would be expected under standard agency principles.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract, by removing the 'no less than reasonable care and discretion' standard, contradicts agency law. The Law Explanation highlights that Restatement (Second) of Agency § 379 implies a standard of care, requiring agents to act with general care and skill. The original text aligns with this, while the changed text allows for a lower standard, potentially violating agency law. Although the scraped snippets do not directly support the answer, the Law Explanation explicitly defines the legal reasoning for the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York applicable to contracts wholly made and performed in such state.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of any state selected by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause to allow the Disclosing Party to unilaterally select the governing law creates an imbalance of power and could be deemed unconscionable if the selected state has no connection to the parties or the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the principles of choice of law under the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187. While parties are generally free to choose the law that governs their contract, this choice is not absolute. Section 187(2) states that the parties' choice of law will not be followed if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction and there is no other reasonable basis for the parties' choice, or if application of the chosen law would be contrary to a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state in the determination of the particular issue. Allowing the Disclosing Party to select any state, regardless of its connection to the contract, undermines these principles and may be deemed unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction because it allows the Disclosing Party to unilaterally select the governing law, potentially choosing a state with no connection to the contract. This contradicts the principles of choice of law, as explained by the LLM, and weakens the requirement that the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally defines conflict of laws as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute, strengthening the argument that a selected state should be logically connected to the contract.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may not be assigned by either party hereto without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be assigned by ADP, LLC, without the prior written consent of the Partner.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for consent for assignment only for ADP creates an imbalance in the agreement, making it potentially unconscionable. The Partner is still bound by the original restriction.
perturbation.law_explanation: While UCC § 2-210 primarily deals with the assignment of contracts for the sale of goods, the underlying principle of fairness in contractual assignments is relevant. By allowing only one party (ADP) to assign the contract without consent, the modification creates an imbalance of power and potentially disadvantages the Partner. This deviates from the implicit understanding of mutual obligations and fairness in contract law, which are intended to prevent undue hardship on one party due to the unilateral actions of the other. The principle of good faith and fair dealing can be invoked to challenge such a provision, especially if it leads to a detrimental outcome for the non-assigning party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-210. Delegation of Performance; Assignment of Rights. (1) A party may perform his duty through a delegate unless otherwise agreed or unless the other party has a substantial interest in having his original promisor perform or control the acts required by the contract . No delegation of performance relieves the party delegating of any duty to perform or any liability for breach. (2) Unless otherwise agreed all rights of either seller or buyer can be assigned except where the assignment would materially change the duty of the other party, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract , or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. A right to damages for breach of the whole contract or a right arising out of the assignor's due performance of his entire obligation can be assigned despite agreement otherwise. (3)Unless the circumstances indicate the contrary a prohibition of assignment of "the contract" is to be construed as barring only the delegation to the assignee of the assignor's performance. (4) An assignment of "the contract" or of "all my rights under the contract" or an assignment in similar general terms is an assignment of rights and unless the language or the circumstances (as in an assignment for security) indicate the contrary, it is a delegation of performance of the duties of the assignor and its acceptance by the assignee constitutes a promise by him to perform those duties. This promise is enforceable by either the assignor or the other party to the original contract . (5) The other party may treat any assignment which delegates performance as creating reasonable grounds for insecurity and may without prejudice to his rights against the assignor demand assurances from the assignee (Section 2-609 ). Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a clear imbalance by allowing only ADP to assign the contract without the Partner's consent, while the Partner remains bound by the original restriction. This contradicts the principle of fairness in contractual assignments, as described in the Law Explanation. Snippet 1, UCC § 2-210, implies that assignment should not materially change the duty of the other party or increase the burden or risk imposed on them. Granting ADP unilateral assignment rights without the Partner's consent potentially does change the Partner's duty and increases their risk, thus creating a contradiction. The Law explanation also invokes the principle of good faith and fair dealing, which is undermined by this unilateral modification.
